1	# Analysis: ACP Tool Registry Compatibility
2	
3	## Scope
4	
5	This analysis answers whether ACP imposes a tool registry pattern that AGH must follow, and which `.resources/*` projects materially use ACP in ways that affect the Tool Registry TechSpec. The research combines official ACP/MCP documentation with read-only subagent passes over `.resources/rayclaw`, `.resources/harnss`, `.resources/acpx`, `.resources/openclaw`, `.resources/opencode`, and an inventory pass across every top-level `.resources/*` project.
6	
7	## Executive Conclusion
8	
9	ACP does not define a durable, programmatic tool registry for callable tools. ACP defines session lifecycle, prompt streaming, client authority callbacks, permission requests, MCP server bootstrap fields, and observable tool-call events. Those tool-call events carry `toolCallId`, `title`, `kind`, `status`, locations, raw input, raw output, and content, but they do not carry a stable `name` field equivalent to MCP `Tool.name`.
10	
11	Therefore, AGH should not model its Tool Registry as an ACP registry, and should not use ACP `title` as a durable policy or collision key.
12	
13	For session-visible AGH tools, the strongest compatibility path remains the accepted MVP path: an AGH-hosted MCP server backed by the daemon Tool Registry. MCP supplies the externally callable `Tool.name`; ACP supplies the way ACP-compatible agents receive `mcpServers`, report tool execution, and request permission.
14	
15	The practical design correction is:
16	
17	- AGH should use one canonical provider-safe `ToolID` everywhere, using reserved double-underscore namespace separators, for example `agh__skill_view`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, or `ext__linear__search`.
18	- The same `ToolID` should be the hosted MCP `Tool.name`; AGH should not introduce a second wire alias in the MVP.
19	- ACP `title` is display-only and event-only.
20	- ACP `ToolKind` is a risk/display hint, not registry identity.
21	- `permissions.mode` remains the session approval ceiling; registry policy remains the granular layer below it.
22	
23	## Official Protocol Constraints
24	
25	### ACP
26	
27	Official ACP schema evidence:
28	
29	- ACP `session/new`, `session/load`, and `session/resume` include `mcpServers`; agents are expected to connect to those MCP servers for the session. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
30	- ACP `ToolCall` is event/reporting data with `toolCallId`, `title`, `kind`, `status`, `rawInput`, `rawOutput`, `locations`, and `content`. The schema describes `title` as human-readable and `toolCallId` as unique within a session. It does not expose a durable callable `name` field. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
31	- ACP `ToolKind` values are coarse categories such as `read`, `edit`, `delete`, `move`, `search`, `execute`, `think`, `fetch`, `switch_mode`, and `other`. The schema says these help clients pick icons and display progress, which is weaker than registry identity. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
32	- ACP `session/request_permission` carries a `toolCall` object plus permission options. It is a permission bridge for a concrete tool call, not a registry discovery API. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>.
33	- The official "ACP Registry" is an agent registry: a catalog of ACP-compatible agents and their install/run metadata, not a callable tool registry. Source: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/registry>.
34	
35	### MCP
36	
37	Official MCP schema evidence:
38	
39	- MCP `tools/list` returns `Tool[]`.
40	- MCP `Tool` has `name`, optional `title`, optional `description`, `inputSchema`, optional `outputSchema`, annotations, execution metadata, and `_meta`.
41	- MCP describes `name` as intended for programmatic/logical use and `title` as intended for UI/end-user contexts. Source: <https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/draft/schema>.
42	
43	Implication: AGH should treat MCP `Tool.name` as the session wire name when exposing AGH registry tools through hosted MCP. ACP does not replace that name.
44	
45	## ACP Usage Inventory Across `.resources/*`
46	
47	| Project | ACP usage | Tool registry relevance |
48	|---|---|---|
49	| `.resources/acpx` | ACP client/orchestrator and conformance tooling. | Has an agent/adapter registry, not a callable tool registry. Passes `mcpServers`; models tool calls by `toolCallId`, title, kind, status, raw input/output. |
50	| `.resources/collaborator-ai` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No registry found. Uses ACP tool update titles for display. |
51	| `.resources/goclaw` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No formal registry. Uses method switches and permission heuristics for ACP callbacks. |
52	| `.resources/harnss` | ACP client/orchestrator with Electron bridge. | Has ACP agent registry and UI rendering adapters, not a tool registry. Converts configured MCP servers to ACP `McpServer[]`. |
53	| `.resources/hermes` | ACP server/agent implementation plus ACP client shim. | Relevant: registers ACP-provided MCP servers into Hermes agent state and valid tool names; maps Hermes tools to ACP `ToolKind` and titles. |
54	| `.resources/multica` | ACP client/orchestrator. | No formal registry. Parses titles such as `terminal:` and `read:` for UI normalization. |
55	| `.resources/openclaw` | ACP server/client/runtime bridge. | Has internal tool catalog and plugin/MCP surfaces, but main ACP bridge does not expose an ACP tool registry and rejects per-session `mcpServers`. |
56	| `.resources/opencode` | Native ACP server. | Has a real internal `ToolRegistry`, but ACP does not expose it as a registry API. Accepts ACP `mcpServers` and converts them into internal MCP config. |
57	| `.resources/paperclip` | Docs/reference only. | Conceptual ACP references only. |
58	| `.resources/rayclaw` | ACP client/orchestrator. | Exposes ACP control as local `acp_*` tools; ACP-reported tool calls are telemetry, not registry entries. |
59	| `.resources/sandbox-agent` | ACP adapter/proxy/client package. | Agent launch registry only; no ACP tool registry found. |
60	| `.resources/t3code` | ACP schema/client/runtime package. | Schema and runtime tracking for ACP tool events; no broad tool registry. |
61	
62	No meaningful ACP evidence was found in `.resources/cc-posts`, `.resources/chat`, `.resources/openfang`, `.resources/pi`, or `.resources/symphony`. `.resources/claude-code` had an `ACP` false positive inside an embedded/base64-like string, not implementation evidence.
63	
64	## Deep Dives
65	
66	### RayClaw
67	
68	RayClaw is an ACP client/orchestrator. It spawns configured ACP agents, runs JSON-RPC lifecycle calls (`initialize`, `session/new`, `session/prompt`, `session/end`), and exposes ACP orchestration to RayClaw's primary LLM through local wrapper tools named `acp_coding`, `acp_new_session`, `acp_prompt`, `acp_end_session`, `acp_list_sessions`, `acp_submit_job`, and `acp_job_status`.
69	
70	Those `acp_*` names are RayClaw's local tool registry convention, not ACP. RayClaw's ACP tool-call handling treats incoming `session/update` tool calls as observations and records them by title/raw input. It does not dispatch those reported ACP tool calls through RayClaw's local registry.
71	
72	Important evidence:
73	
74	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:436-453` initializes ACP with client capabilities, not a host tool registry.
75	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:752-843` handles `session/request_permission`.
76	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:846-984` parses ACP tool-call progress.
77	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs:1521-1531` creates sessions with `mcpServers: []`.
78	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/tools/acp.rs:16-40` registers the local `acp_*` wrapper tools.
79	- `.resources/rayclaw/tests/acp_integration.rs:128-240` enforces local tool-name uniqueness, allowed characters, length, and collision checks.
80	
81	Transferable points:
82	
83	- Separate ACP orchestration tools from normal runtime tools.
84	- Treat ACP tool calls as child-agent telemetry unless AGH deliberately bridges them.
85	- Do not prefer `allow_always` as an automatic approval default the way RayClaw does under `auto_approve`; AGH should keep durable grants explicit.
86	- Reject or disambiguate sanitized name collisions rather than truncating.
87	
88	### Harnss
89	
90	Harnss is an ACP client/orchestrator with an Electron bridge and React UI. It converts renderer MCP server configs into ACP SDK `McpServer[]`, including stdio and remote transports, then passes them into `newSession` and `loadSession`. It also supports live reload through ACP `loadSession` when available.
91	
92	Harnss does not consume or expose an ACP tool registry. Its "registry" evidence is an ACP agent registry and a UI-side static MCP renderer table. ACP tool calls are converted into UI messages keyed by `toolCallId`, using title/kind/raw input/output normalization.
93	
94	Important evidence:
95	
96	- `.resources/harnss/electron/src/ipc/acp-sessions.ts:193-215` converts MCP configs to ACP `McpServer[]`.
97	- `.resources/harnss/electron/src/ipc/acp-sessions.ts:365-483` wires ACP connection callbacks, event forwarding, and permission bridge.
98	- `.resources/harnss/electron/src/ipc/acp-sessions.ts:521-546` starts ACP sessions with MCP servers.
99	- `.resources/harnss/electron/src/ipc/acp-sessions.ts:793-828` reloads sessions with MCP servers.
100	- `.resources/harnss/src/hooks/useACP.ts:194-337` converts ACP tool events into UI messages.
101	- `.resources/harnss/src/hooks/useACP.ts:413-473` handles ACP permission requests.
102	- `.resources/harnss/src/lib/engine/acp-adapter.ts:267-358` derives display/tool renderer names from ACP title/kind/raw input.
103	- `.resources/harnss/src/components/McpToolContent.tsx:83-138` supports SDK-style `mcp__Server__tool` names and ACP-style `Tool: Server/tool` titles in UI rendering.
104	
105	Transferable points:
106	
107	- Normalize ACP event data at the boundary into AGH's canonical tool-call observation model.
108	- Preserve raw ACP permission options; do not collapse manual allow/deny into "first allow" or "first reject" if the protocol provides multiple option IDs.
109	- Keep rendering names separate from policy names.
110	- Pass MCP servers as session bootstrap/load data when AGH chooses per-session MCP support.
111	
112	### ACPX
113	
114	ACPX is a headless ACP client/orchestrator. Its registry is an agent/adapter registry mapping names like `codex`, `claude`, `gemini`, and others to launch commands. This is not a callable tool registry.
115	
116	ACPX implements client authority callbacks such as filesystem read/write, terminal create/output/wait/kill/release, and `session/request_permission`. It parses `mcpServers` from config and passes them through to `session/new` and `session/load`. It does not discover or normalize MCP tools into a registry.
117	
118	Important evidence:
119	
120	- `.resources/acpx/src/agent-registry.ts:38-107` maps adapter names to commands.
121	- `.resources/acpx/src/mcp-servers.ts:100-177` parses MCP server configs.
122	- `.resources/acpx/src/acp/client.ts:475-538` wires ACP client callbacks and initialize capabilities.
123	- `.resources/acpx/src/acp/client.ts:638-693` passes `mcpServers` to `session/new` and `session/load`.
124	- `.resources/acpx/src/permissions.ts:98-152` implements coarse permission decisions.
125	- `.resources/acpx/src/session/conversation-model.ts:310-353` persists tool events keyed by tool call ID.
126	- `.resources/acpx/conformance/cases/021-prompt-post-success-drain.json:1-50` shows late tool updates can arrive after prompt success.
127	
128	Transferable points:
129	
130	- Keep agent/provider registries separate from Tool Registry.
131	- ACP compatibility includes filesystem and terminal callbacks where advertised; those callbacks must share AGH's registry policy engine or be routed through equivalent approval gates.
132	- Preserve distinct identities: AGH record IDs, ACP session IDs, provider-native session IDs, tool call IDs, and registry tool IDs.
133	- Add a protocol-aware drain/settle window for late `tool_call_update` events.
134	
135	### OpenClaw
136	
137	OpenClaw's main `openclaw acp` bridge is a Gateway-backed ACP server. It forwards prompts to the Gateway and translates Gateway events into ACP session updates. The main bridge advertises MCP HTTP/SSE support as disabled and rejects non-empty per-session `mcpServers`; its docs say MCP should be configured at the Gateway/agent layer.
138	
139	OpenClaw has rich internal tool catalogs and plugin/MCP surfaces, but the main ACP bridge does not expose them as an ACP tool registry. Tool identity in ACP is display/event identity: title formatting plus inferred `ToolKind`.
140	
141	Important evidence:
142	
143	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/server.ts:4-13` and `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/server.ts:104-122` bootstrap the ACP stdio server.
144	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/translator.ts:519-540` advertises ACP capabilities.
145	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/translator.ts:542-603` handles session creation/loading.
146	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/translator.ts:1417-1424` rejects non-empty `mcpServers`.
147	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/translator.ts:848-940` maps Gateway tool events to ACP tool updates.
148	- `.resources/openclaw/src/acp/event-mapper.ts:297-342` formats tool titles and infers tool kind.
149	- `.resources/openclaw/src/agents/tool-catalog.ts:20-37` and `.resources/openclaw/src/agents/tool-catalog.ts:306-393` define a separate internal tool catalog.
150	- `.resources/openclaw/extensions/acpx/src/runtime-internals/mcp-proxy.mjs:33-64` shows the ACPX extension can inject MCP servers into embedded ACP sessions, unlike the main gateway bridge.
151	
152	Transferable points:
153	
154	- AGH must explicitly choose whether its ACP bridge accepts per-session `mcpServers` like OpenCode/Harnss/ACPX or rejects them like OpenClaw's gateway bridge.
155	- If AGH supports both runtime-managed MCP and ACP-provided MCP servers, precedence and collision rules must be explicit.
156	- Do not use substring heuristics for registry policy where explicit tool metadata is available.
157	
158	### OpenCode
159	
160	OpenCode implements a native ACP server and has a real internal `ToolRegistry`. This is the strongest local reference for how an agent can maintain a rich internal registry while ACP still sees only session lifecycle, MCP bootstrap, tool-call updates, and permission requests.
161	
162	OpenCode accepts ACP per-session `mcpServers`, stores them in ACP session state, converts them into internal MCP config, and adds them through its SDK. Its internal MCP naming pattern exposes MCP tools as `sanitize(server) + "_" + sanitize(tool)` while preserving the original MCP tool name for the actual call.
163	
164	Important evidence:
165	
166	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/acp.ts:23-60` bootstraps `opencode acp`.
167	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/types.ts:1-16` and `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/session.ts:8-75` store ACP session state, including `mcpServers`.
168	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/agent.ts:534-578` advertises MCP support.
169	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/agent.ts:584-687` accepts MCP servers on session creation/loading.
170	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/agent.ts:1216-1254` converts ACP MCP servers into internal MCP config.
171	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/mcp/index.ts:115-146` and `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/mcp/index.ts:618-651` implement sanitized server/tool naming while preserving raw MCP names.
172	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/tool/tool.ts:34-43` and `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/tool/registry.ts:163-207` define internal tool definitions and registry behavior.
173	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/agent.ts:273-455` emits ACP tool-call lifecycle updates.
174	- `.resources/opencode/packages/opencode/src/acp/agent.ts:190-271` bridges internal permission events to ACP `session/request_permission`.
175	
176	Transferable points:
177	
178	- Keep AGH's internal registry richer than ACP.
179	- Store ACP-provided MCP servers in session state, not global daemon config.
180	- Preserve raw MCP server/tool names separately from the canonical AGH `ToolID`.
181	- Emit a stable ACP lifecycle, preferably `pending -> in_progress -> completed/failed`, even when the underlying runtime first reports a running event.
182	- Do not rely on a single-underscore sanitized naming scheme without collision diagnostics.
183	
184	### Hermes, Multica, GoClaw, Sandbox-Agent, T3Code
185	
186	These projects reinforce the same split:
187	
188	- `.resources/hermes` is relevant because it registers ACP-provided MCP servers into agent state and valid tool names, then maps tool events into ACP kinds/titles. It has useful registry ideas, but ACP remains the session/event layer.
189	- `.resources/multica` and `.resources/goclaw` normalize ACP tool titles/kinds for display and permission heuristics; neither shows a protocol-level tool registry.
190	- `.resources/sandbox-agent` has ACP HTTP-to-stdio adapter and launch registry logic, but no callable ACP tool registry.
191	- `.resources/t3code` provides ACP schema/client/runtime tracking for tool-call events, not a broad registry.
192	
193	## Design Implications For AGH
194	
195	1. ACP compatibility is not a reason to avoid a daemon Tool Registry. ACP leaves tool discovery/execution models to the agent/runtime, or to MCP servers supplied to the session.
196	
197	2. AGH should expose daemon-owned session tools through an AGH-hosted MCP server in the MVP. This matches the accepted ADR-002 direction and aligns with ACP's `mcpServers` field.
198	
199	3. The registry's canonical ID must not be ACP `title`. Use one stable provider-safe `ToolID` across AGH and hosted MCP.
200	
201	4. The registry should store one canonical callable identity plus metadata:
202	   - `ToolID`: provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved `__`, for example `agh__skill_view`.
203	   - `DisplayTitle`: user-facing title only.
204	   - `SourceRef`: structured provenance, for example built-in, MCP server, extension ID, bundle ID, provider ID.
205	
206	5. Collision handling must be fail-closed:
207	   - Canonical `ToolID` collision: provider registration error or conflicted diagnostic.
208	   - Sanitized external-name collision: tool is not exposed to the session until disambiguated.
209	   - Display title collision: allowed, because titles are not policy identities.
210	
211	6. Operator and session projections should remain separate:
212	   - Operator surfaces show unavailable, unauthorized, and conflicted tools with reason codes.
213	   - Session/model surfaces expose only callable tools after availability, authorization, approval ceiling, and collision checks.
214	
215	7. ACP permission policy integration must remain ceiling-based:
216	   - `deny-all` denies by default.
217	   - `approve-reads` auto-approves only registry-classified read-only tools and ACP read/search callbacks AGH classifies as read-only.
218	   - `approve-all` skips approval prompts for otherwise allowed tools, but does not bypass registry deny rules, extension grants, session lineage, source trust, availability, hooks, or conflict checks.
219	
220	8. ACP filesystem and terminal callbacks, if AGH advertises them, must not bypass Tool Registry policy. Either route them through the registry as first-class built-in tools or share the same policy/approval engine with equivalent telemetry and hooks.
221	
222	9. ACP `ToolKind` should be explicit metadata on AGH descriptors. Heuristics from title/kind are fallback-only for external ACP events that AGH observes but does not own.
223	
224	10. AGH should persist observed ACP tool calls separately from registry definitions. Observations are keyed by `toolCallId` within a session and carry title/kind/status/raw input/output. Registry entries are keyed by canonical `ToolID`.
225	
226	11. AGH should support late tool-call updates after prompt completion by draining the ACP event stream for a bounded window or until protocol-specific completion conditions are met.
227	
228	12. AGH should decide explicitly whether to accept third-party ACP `mcpServers` from clients:
229	    - If accepted, store them as session-scoped tool sources with clear precedence and conflict policy.
230	    - If rejected, document the OpenClaw-style stance and require MCP sources to be configured through AGH's registry/config lifecycle.
231	    - For this TechSpec, the safer MVP path is AGH-managed hosted MCP first, with acceptance of client-supplied MCP servers as a compatibility extension only if collision and source-trust rules are implemented.
232	
233	## Accepted Naming And Collision Recommendation
234	
235	Adopt one canonical public `ToolID` format:
236	
237	- Canonical ID: provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved double underscore, for example `agh__skill_view`, `agh__tool_search`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, `ext__linear__search`.
238	- Hosted MCP `Tool.name`: same as the canonical `ToolID`.
239	- Display title: human-readable and non-unique, for example `View Skill`.
240	- Source/provenance: structured fields, not inferred solely from name prefixes.
241	- No shadowing: providers cannot replace an existing canonical ID unless they are the same source updating the same record.
242	- No silent truncation: if sanitization or length rules would collide, registration/session projection marks the tool conflicted and hides it from the session surface.
243	- Policy, dispatch, telemetry, hooks, CLI, HTTP, UDS, and hosted MCP all use the same `ToolID`.
244	
245	This keeps AGH's internal registry expressive while respecting MCP wire compatibility and ACP's event-oriented model.
246	
247	## Evidence Summary
248	
249	Official protocol sources:
250	
251	- ACP schema: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/schema>
252	- ACP agent registry: <https://agentclientprotocol.com/registry>
253	- MCP draft schema: <https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/draft/schema>
254	
255	Primary local evidence:
256	
257	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/acp.rs`
258	- `.resources/rayclaw/src/tools/acp.rs`
259	- `.resources/rayclaw/tests/acp_integration.rs`
260	- `.resources/harnss/electron/src/ipc/acp-sessions.ts`
261	- `.resources/harnss/src/hooks/useACP.ts`
262	- `.resources/harnss/src/lib/engine/acp-adapter.ts`
263	- `.resources/acpx/src/acp/client.ts`
264	- `.resources/acpx/src/mcp-servers.ts`
265	- `.resources/acpx/src/permissions.ts`

1	# AGH Current State: Tool Registry Foundation
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	AGH already has the cold side of a tool catalog, but not the runtime side.
6	`internal/tools` defines a canonical `tool` resource shape and the daemon projects tool records through the generic resources system. Extensions can publish static tool metadata from `extension.toml`. Sessions can also carry concrete permission atoms for tools in lineage metadata.
7	
8	What is missing is the executable registry: a central service that can answer which tools exist for a specific agent/session, whether each tool is available now, whether the caller is allowed to use it, and how to dispatch the call through one uniform pipeline.
9	
10	This matters because AGH's product premise is agent-first manageability. A tool registry is incomplete if it only helps internal Go code list metadata or if each ACP runtime owns a private tool universe that AGH cannot inspect, govern, or extend.
11	
12	## Existing Mechanisms
13	
14	### Tool resource metadata
15	
16	`internal/tools/tool.go` defines `ToolSource` values for `builtin`, `mcp`, `extension`, and `dynamic`, plus a small `Tool` record:
17	
18	- `Name`
19	- `Description`
20	- `InputSchema`
21	- `ReadOnly`
22	- `Source`
23	
24	The only provider interface is:
25	
26	```go
27	type ToolProvider interface {
28	    Tools(ctx context.Context) ([]Tool, error)
29	}
30	```
31	
32	There is no `Call`, `Availability`, `CheckPermission`, `Aliases`, `Namespace`, `IsConcurrencySafe`, `IsDestructive`, `MaxResultBytes`, `Owner`, `Visibility`, or provenance-rich source metadata.
33	
34	### Desired-state resources
35	
36	`internal/tools/resource.go` defines `ToolResourceKind = "tool"` and validates tool records as JSON-object specs with a maximum size of 256 KiB. This is a good base for persisted inventory, desired-state reconciliation, and extension-published tool metadata.
37	
38	The daemon already has a generic `resourceCatalog[T]` in `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go` and a `newToolProjector` that projects reconciled `tool` records into a daemon-local snapshot. This catalog is descriptive and revisioned, but it is not executable.
39	
40	### Extension-published tools
41	
42	`internal/extension/manifest.go` already lets extensions declare:
43	
44	```go
45	type ResourcesConfig struct {
46	    Tools map[string]ToolConfig `toml:"tools,omitempty" json:"tools,omitempty"`
47	    MCPServers map[string]MCPServerConfig `toml:"mcp_servers,omitempty" json:"mcp_servers,omitempty"`
48	}
49	```
50	
51	`ToolConfig` carries description, input schema, and read-only status. `ResolveManifestToolResources` converts these manifest entries into `toolspkg.Tool` records with `Source = ToolSourceExtension`. The daemon syncer publishes them into the resource graph with source keys like `extension/<name>/tool/<tool>`.
52	
53	This is close to OpenClaw's manifest-first model, but AGH currently stops at metadata. A manifest-declared extension tool is not callable unless some separate ACP/MCP/runtime surface happens to expose it.
54	
55	### MCP resources
56	
57	The same extension manifest can declare MCP servers, and the daemon sync path resolves them into desired-state MCP server resources. Skills can also declare MCP sidecars. This gives AGH a strong candidate adapter for extension tools: manifest tools may be backed by an MCP server, an extension sidecar Host API endpoint, or native AGH built-ins, but all should normalize into one registry contract.
58	
59	### Session permission atoms
60	
61	`internal/store/session_lineage.go` defines `SessionPermissionPolicy` with concrete atoms:
62	
63	- `Tools`
64	- `Skills`
65	- `MCPServers`
66	- `WorkspacePaths`
67	- `NetworkChannels`
68	- `SandboxProfiles`
69	
70	`internal/session/spawn.go` validates child permissions as a subset of parent permissions. This is an important base for runtime tool policy because it is already persisted with session lineage and already participates in spawn delegation.
71	
72	The current agent definition has a flat `Tools []string` field in `internal/config/agent.go`. It lacks allow/deny overlays, named toolsets, namespace patterns, visibility tiers, and risk classes.
73	
74	### Hooks around tool calls
75	
76	`internal/hooks/payloads.go` already defines `ToolPreCallPayload`, `ToolPostCallPayload`, `ToolPostErrorPayload`, `ToolCallPatch`, and `ToolResultPatch`. This is the right policy extension point for a centralized dispatch pipeline:
77	
78	- pre-call hooks can deny or mutate input
79	- post-call hooks can redact or mutate output
80	- post-error hooks can classify or recover failures
81	
82	The current gap is that AGH does not have a single dispatch pipeline that all AGH-owned tools must pass through.
83	
84	### Skills registry contrast
85	
86	`internal/skills.Registry` is much more mature than tools. It has global snapshots, workspace overlays, content loading, verification, install provenance, and `GlobalVersion()` for invalidation. Skills are injected as a static prompt catalog at session start, while tool resources are not exposed as a session-callable registry.
87	
88	The Tool Registry should copy the skills registry's useful properties where they fit: global/workspace overlays, versioned snapshots, resource projection, progressive disclosure, and explicit content/schema loading.
89	
90	## Gaps
91	
92	1. No central runtime registry that owns executable tool handles.
93	2. No agent-facing discovery API for tools.
94	3. No native AGH tools such as `agh__tool_search`, `agh__skill_view`, or `agh__network_send`.
95	4. No availability model for env vars, binaries, MCP health, extension health, workspace scope, or policy state.
96	5. No central permission pipeline for AGH-owned tools.
97	6. No namespace or structured provenance model, so duplicate names would be ambiguous.
98	7. No toolsets or bundles comparable to skills/capabilities.
99	8. No direct extension execution boundary for manifest-declared tools.
100	9. No consistent way to expose tools over CLI, HTTP, UDS, and session-visible agent surfaces.
101	10. No usage telemetry by tool or skill.
102	11. No result-size budget, persistence policy, redaction, or output mapping at registry level.
103	12. The `dynamic` source enum exists but has no producer.
104	
105	## Relevant Code Paths
106	
107	- `internal/tools/tool.go:14-136`: tool source enum, metadata-only `Tool`, and list-only `ToolProvider`.
108	- `internal/tools/resource.go:13-61`: `ToolResourceKind` codec and JSON schema validation.
109	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:20-122`: generic daemon `resourceCatalog` and `newToolProjector`.
110	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:620-640`: extension manifest tools are projected into desired-state resources.
111	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:55-62`: extension resources can include tools and MCP servers.
112	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:154-160`: extension `ToolConfig` is static metadata only.
113	- `internal/extension/resource_publication.go:13-31`: manifest tool declarations become `toolspkg.Tool` records.
114	- `internal/config/agent.go:14-23`: `AgentDef.Tools []string` is flat.
115	- `internal/store/session_lineage.go:31-39`: session lineage has concrete `Tools` permission atoms.
116	- `internal/session/interfaces.go:244-251`: `AgentDriver` has no catalog-delta or AGH tool injection extension.
117	- `internal/hooks/payloads.go:520-568`: tool pre/post/error payloads already exist.
118	- `internal/skills/registry.go:100-103`: skills expose a global version suitable for catalog delta detection.
119	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md`: prior autonomy gap analysis with G1-G12 and P1-P9 proposals.
120	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md`: security gaps relevant once AGH exposes agent-callable tools.
121	
122	## Design Constraints for the TechSpec
123	
124	The Tool Registry should be a foundation, not a pile of built-in commands. It should define the contracts, policy path, extension boundary, and surfaces first, then add a small bootstrap set of native AGH tools to prove the system.
125	
126	AGH should avoid copying in-process plugin patterns from Python/TypeScript systems. Third-party executable tools should cross a process/protocol boundary: MCP, extension sidecar Host API, subprocess adapter, or future bridge SDK. Built-in Go tools can register in-process because they are part of the daemon binary.
127	
128	The cold `tool` resource should remain valuable as catalog metadata and desired state, but executable dispatch must be modeled separately. A manifest-declared tool can be installed and discoverable while still being unavailable until its backend is healthy and authorized.
129	
130	Availability and authorization must both be rechecked at dispatch time. Hiding unavailable tools from discovery is useful, but it is not a security boundary.
131	
132	## Open Questions
133	
134	1. Should extension tools be executable in the MVP, or should MVP only make them discoverable with an explicit unavailable state?
135	2. If executable, should extension tools be allowed only through MCP/sidecar boundaries, or should trusted bundled extensions get in-process handlers?
136	3. Should the first AGH-native tool surface be injected into ACP sessions, exposed as an MCP server hosted by AGH, or exposed only through CLI/UDS/HTTP for drivers to call indirectly?
137	4. Should `internal/catalog` coordinate tools and skills, or should `internal/tools` own runtime tools while a thinner catalog/search service composes skills and tools?
138	5. Which visibility tiers are needed for MVP: internal, CLI/HTTP, agent-visible, model-visible, deferred-discoverable, extension-private?
139	
140	## Evidence
141	
142	- `internal/tools/tool.go:91-136`: current `Tool` and `ToolProvider` are descriptive and list-only.
143	- `internal/tools/resource.go:13-61`: tool resource codec validates metadata records.
144	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:55-62`: extension manifests can publish `resources.tools`.
145	- `internal/extension/resource_publication.go:13-31`: extension manifest tools become static tool resources.
146	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:620-640`: daemon sync publishes extension tools and MCP servers into resource desired state.
147	- `internal/store/session_lineage.go:31-39`: session permission policy already includes `Tools`.
148	- `internal/hooks/payloads.go:520-568`: tool lifecycle hooks are already typed.
149	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md:1-220`: prior gap analysis identifies no runtime tool registry, no discovery API, no availability, and no agent-facing skill/tool call surface.
150	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md:1-140`: security analysis warns that URL-capable, command-capable, MCP, and skill-install surfaces require stronger guardrails before broad exposure.
151

1	# Analysis: `mark3labs/mcp-go` Adoption
2	
3	## Scope
4	
5	This analysis evaluates whether the Tool Registry foundation should implement MCP protocol/server/client behavior manually inside AGH, adopt the official Go SDK, or adopt `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` while keeping AGH-specific policy/auth/manageability logic above the protocol layer.
6	
7	The research uses primary sources only:
8	
9	- repository and releases: `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go`
10	- locally downloaded module source resolved by `go list -m -json github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go@latest`
11	- official Go SDK repository used only for comparison: `github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk`
12	- current AGH MCP code under `internal/mcp`, `internal/config`, `internal/acp`, and daemon MCP resource publication paths
13	
14	## Executive Conclusion
15	
16	AGH should adopt `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go`, specifically module version `v0.49.0` released on April 21, 2026, for MCP protocol/session/transport mechanics instead of planning a hand-rolled MCP implementation in the Tool Registry workstream.
17	
18	Compared with the official Go SDK, `mcp-go` fits AGH's hosted-proxy and daemon-owned call-through needs better in three places:
19	
20	- it has first-class `stdio`, `streamable HTTP`, and `SSE` client surfaces;
21	- it has direct hosted-server ergonomics for session-scoped tool mutation and `tools/list_changed`;
22	- it exposes `RawInputSchema` and `RawOutputSchema` on `mcp.Tool`, which is a better match for AGH's descriptor-authored hosted schemas.
23	
24	This does **not** mean handing MCP ownership to the SDK wholesale. AGH must keep these boundaries:
25	
26	- `internal/tools` remains the registry/policy/provenance/decision owner.
27	- `internal/mcp/auth` remains the durable OAuth + redacted-status owner.
28	- `internal/daemon` remains the composition root and hosted-MCP session binder.
29	- AGH-specific `ToolID`, approval bridge, UDS peer validation, reason codes, redaction rules, and manageability surfaces remain AGH-owned.
30	
31	The library should replace **manual MCP protocol plumbing**, not AGH policy or AGH auth/state lifecycles.
32	
33	## What `mark3labs/mcp-go` Already Covers
34	
35	### 1. Server and client lifecycle
36	
37	`mcp-go` already exposes the main shapes AGH needs for hosted MCP and remote call-through:
38	
39	- hosted server creation through `server.NewMCPServer(...)`;
40	- concrete client lifecycle through `client.NewClient(...)`, `Initialize(...)`, request methods, and `Close()`;
41	- built-in session-aware server features such as session-specific tools and list-change signaling.
42	
43	This is a better fit than a custom JSON-RPC layer because the library already maps MCP lifecycle behavior into those abstractions.
44	
45	### 2. Stdio, streamable HTTP, and SSE transports
46	
47	The library already supports:
48	
49	- `server.ServeStdio(...)` and `client.NewStdioMCPClient(...)`;
50	- `server.NewStreamableHTTPServer(...)` and `client.NewStreamableHttpClient(...)`;
51	- `server.NewSSEServer(...)` and `client.NewSSEMCPClient(...)`.
52	
53	That maps directly to AGH's two main MCP paths:
54	
55	- hosted AGH MCP proxy over stdio for ACP session injection;
56	- daemon-owned MCP clients for configured stdio, remote HTTP, and remote SSE MCP servers.
57	
58	Important scope implication for AGH: the library has a distinct SSE client path. That means the prior hard-cut of `mcp_server.transport = "sse"` was an artifact of the official SDK choice, not a product requirement. If AGH wants to preserve its current MCP config surface, `mcp-go` supports that direction directly.
59	
60	### 3. Tool registration, schema validation, and `tools/list_changed`
61	
62	The library already supports server-side tool registration and client-side tool calling:
63	
64	- `server.AddTool` / `AddTools` / `SetTools` / `DeleteTools`
65	- `client.ListTools`
66	- `client.CallTool`
67	- automatic `notifications/tools/list_changed`
68	- session-specific tools through `AddSessionTool` / `AddSessionTools`
69	
70	The library also exposes raw schema fields on `mcp.Tool`:
71	
72	- `RawInputSchema`
73	- `RawOutputSchema`
74	
75	That removes a large amount of otherwise error-prone manual glue in AGH's hosted MCP proxy, where descriptor-owned schema bytes should remain authoritative.
76	
77	### 4. Optional OAuth helpers, but not auth ownership
78	
79	The library ships client-side OAuth helpers:
80	
81	- `client.NewOAuthStreamableHttpClient(...)`
82	- `client.NewOAuthSSEClient(...)`
83	- `transport.OAuthConfig`
84	- `transport.TokenStore`
85	- `transport.OAuthHandler`
86	
87	However, `transport.NewOAuthHandler` defaults to `MemoryTokenStore` if no store is supplied. That is not compatible with AGH's current auth ownership model.
88	
89	## What AGH Must Keep Owning
90	
91	### 1. Durable MCP auth lifecycle
92	
93	AGH already has real MCP auth product surfaces:
94	
95	- `internal/mcp/auth`
96	- `internal/store/globaldb` token persistence
97	- `agh mcp auth login/status/logout`
98	- settings/API redacted `auth_status`
99	
100	The library's OAuth helpers are useful protocol helpers, but they do not replace AGH's persistent token store, operator workflows, or redacted diagnostics. The right design is:
101	
102	- keep AGH auth state authoritative;
103	- prefer AGH-owned header injection or a narrow AGH-owned adapter inside `internal/mcp`;
104	- never let `MemoryTokenStore`, library-owned login flows, or library-owned refresh persistence become the authority.
105	
106	### 2. Registry policy, provenance, and reason codes
107	
108	The library is intentionally protocol-level. It does not implement AGH-specific:
109	
110	- canonical `ToolID` policy;
111	- operator/session projection split;
112	- source trust rules;
113	- lineage subset enforcement;
114	- approval bridge semantics;
115	- AGH reason-code taxonomy;
116	- result redaction and cross-surface consistency requirements.
117	
118	Those remain in AGH's registry layer.
119	
120	### 3. Hosted MCP bind/authentication model
121	
122	AGH's hosted MCP server is not just an MCP server. It is a daemon-scoped exposure transport bound to:
123	
124	- a session,
125	- a workspace,
126	- a UDS peer,
127	- an expected AGH binary,
128	- a daemon-minted bind nonce,
129	- AGH approval semantics.
130	
131	The library can run the MCP protocol over stdio, but AGH still owns the pre-bind/bind rules and the UDS hop into `Registry.Call`.
132	
133	## Relevant Library Limits / Rough Edges
134	
135	### 1. Remote schema fidelity is not byte-exact by default
136	
137	Hosted MCP can preserve exact descriptor bytes because AGH can populate `RawInputSchema` and `RawOutputSchema` directly.
138	
139	External MCP discovery is more nuanced. The client path decodes `tools/list` into typed `mcp.Tool` values. When upstream schemas arrive only through structured fields, AGH should treat a canonical JSON encoding of the decoded schema as authoritative rather than promising byte-for-byte preservation of the original upstream payload.
140	
141	This is not a blocker, but it should be stated accurately in the TechSpec.
142	
143	### 2. Library OAuth is not the AGH operator surface
144	
145	The library can perform OAuth-oriented transport setup, but AGH needs:
146	
147	- durable storage,
148	- redacted diagnostics,
149	- CLI/settings/API manageability,
150	- explicit ownership boundaries.
151	
152	Therefore the library OAuth package should be considered optional or adapter-facing, not the new source of truth.
153	
154	### 3. Streamable HTTP resumability is not implemented
155	
156	The streamable HTTP implementation explicitly does not support stream resumability on client or server.
157	
158	That matters for any future AGH long-lived remote listening path, but it is not a blocker for the MVP hosted stdio path or on-demand remote discovery/call path.
159	
160	### 4. Continuous listening should remain optional in MVP
161	
162	The streamable HTTP client can enable standalone GET listening through `WithContinuousListening()`. AGH does not need that as a hard dependency for MVP. If enabled later, external `notifications/tools/list_changed` should still be treated only as cache invalidation hints, not as authoritative registry mutations.
163	
164	## Recommendation
165	
166	Adopt `mcp-go` in these exact places:
167	
168	1. Hosted AGH MCP stdio proxy:
169	   - use `server.NewMCPServer`
170	   - register session-callable registry tools as `mcp.Tool` values with `RawInputSchema` / `RawOutputSchema`
171	   - serve over `server.ServeStdio`
172	
173	2. Daemon-owned MCP clients for configured servers:
174	   - stdio MCP servers via `client.NewStdioMCPClient`
175	   - remote HTTP MCP servers via `client.NewStreamableHttpClient`
176	   - remote SSE MCP servers via `client.NewSSEMCPClient`
177	
178	3. Keep AGH auth as the durable owner:
179	   - `internal/mcp/auth` stays authoritative
180	   - outbound requests use AGH-owned auth state, with header injection or a narrow internal adapter
181	   - avoid `client.NewOAuthStreamableHttpClient`, `client.NewOAuthSSEClient`, default `transport.NewOAuthHandler`, and `MemoryTokenStore` as product authority
182	
183	4. Keep AGH registry semantics outside the SDK:
184	   - projection, policy, provenance, approval, hook dispatch, telemetry, redaction, and reason codes stay AGH-owned
185	
186	## TechSpec Implications
187	
188	The TechSpec should stop implying a hand-rolled MCP protocol server/client stack. It should instead state:
189	
190	- AGH uses `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` for MCP protocol lifecycle, tools, and stdio/HTTP/SSE transports.
191	- AGH wraps the library inside `internal/mcp` and `agh tool mcp`; the library is not exposed as the policy authority.
192	- AGH keeps durable auth in `internal/mcp/auth`.
193	- AGH canonical `ToolID` remains the hosted MCP `Tool.name`.
194	- Hosted schemas are exact descriptor bytes; external discovered schemas are preserved as raw bytes when available or canonicalized decoded schema otherwise.
195	- Remote `sse` remains a valid MCP transport in config and resources when the source actually declares SSE.
196	
197	## Evidence

1	# L-005 — Authoritative primitives are exclusive
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy ADR-004); reinforced 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_11)
5	**Evidence sources:** ADR-004 + task_11 memory + global_runs analysis
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The mechanical scheduler in `internal/scheduler` was tempted to claim runs directly during sweeps and recoveries. That would have collapsed the agent-pull model into a daemon-push model and given two components authority over the same state transition.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	When two components can perform the same authoritative state transition (claim, spawn, migrate, narrow), they will eventually disagree. Either you serialize them (introducing locks, latency, and complexity), or you accept two sources of truth (introducing races and recovery bugs). The clean answer is to pick one authority per transition and force everything else to _observe and notify_ rather than _act_.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> When an authoritative primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate the transition. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not.
18	
19	## Examples (canonical authorities in AGH)
20	
21	| Transition                    | Authority                            | Allowed peers                                                  |
22	| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
23	| Claim a `task_run`            | `task.Service.ClaimNextRun`          | `internal/scheduler` may wake idle agents; never claims itself |
24	| Spawn a child session         | Daemon-managed safe-spawn API        | Coordinator submits requests; the daemon decides               |
25	| Apply a schema migration      | `internal/store` migrations registry | `EnsureSchema` is forbidden for column changes                 |
26	| Mutate session terminal state | Session manager                      | Channels, hooks, observability emit events but cannot mutate   |
27	| Approve / publish a task      | Operator + manual API surface        | Coordinators receive enqueues; do not auto-approve             |
28	
29	## Operationalization
30	
31	- **Scheduler can wake and sweep, but cannot claim.** `internal/scheduler` issues `scheduler.wake.count`/`no_match`/`lease_sweep.count`/`error` metrics — never `task.run.claim.success`.
32	- **Hooks can deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety invariants** (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
33	- **Coordination channels are NEVER an ownership/status authority.** Channel `status`/`result` messages cannot mutate ownership/terminal state.
34	
35	## Anti-pattern
36	
37	- Adding a "scheduler.\*" hook taxonomy that lets external code claim runs.
38	- Letting the coordinator bypass `ClaimNextRun` through a "fast-path" for trusted runs.
39	- Allowing the network layer to write terminal state via channel messages.
40	
41	## Source
42	
43	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
44	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-009.md` — Hooks First-Class (forbids hook-as-claim)
45	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_11.md` — scheduler scope-creep avoided
46	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L4
47	
```

1	# L-006 — Greenfield + zero-legacy means _delete_, not _adapt_
2	
3	**Class:** Project posture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-17 (harness TechSpec review, Portuguese-language reviewer)
5	**Evidence sources:** Harness review + `remove-legacy-alpha.md` standing directive + multiple ADRs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The harness TechSpec proposed migrating an `inputAugmenter` callback to a `TurnAugmenter` pipeline. The spec did not say whether the old callback was deleted, kept as an adapter, or coexisting. The reviewer (in Portuguese) flagged this directly: _"política zero-legacy exige declarar 'delete'"_ — the zero-legacy policy _requires_ the spec to declare what is deleted.
10	
11	This is a stronger application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule: it's not enough to _allow_ deletion; specs must _enumerate_ what is deleted.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	When a spec says "we are migrating to X" without naming the delete-target, agents default to keeping both. Compatibility shims, adapters, and "preserve old behavior" branches accumulate as technical debt. Greenfield discipline only works if every breaking-change spec explicitly names what disappears.
16	
17	## Rule
18	
19	> Every breaking-change techspec MUST explicitly name its delete targets. "Delete the old thing" is not a default; it is a checklist item that must be enumerated.
20	
21	## Operationalization
22	
23	In every TechSpec that changes a public surface (or any meaningful internal contract), include a section like:
24	
25	```markdown
26	## Delete Targets
27	
28	- `internal/foo.OldType` (replaced by `internal/foo.NewType` in step 3)
29	- `pkg/bar.LegacyAdapter` (no callers after migration; remove in step 5)
30	- TOML key `[old.section]` (renamed; no backward alias)
31	- HTTP endpoint `/v0/old/path` (replaced by `/v1/new/path`; no redirect)
32	```
33	
34	Renames sweep code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, AND `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields, no migration code.
35	
36	## Allowed exception (single-pass repair)
37	
38	When the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite," in-place ALTER + one-shot repair is allowed if and only if:
39	
40	1. Repair is bounded to a single boot.
41	2. Strict semantics resume immediately after repair.
42	3. The exception is documented in an ADR.
43	
44	Reference: `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`.
45	
46	## Source
47	
48	- `.codex/plans/remove-legacy-alpha.md` (standing directive)
49	- `.codex/sessions/2026/04/17/.../exec-20260417-232547-929722000/turns/0001/response.txt` (harness review, Portuguese)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-001.md`
51	- Multiple `network-rename`, `assistant-ui-hard-cut`, `workspace-menu-hardcut` plans in `.codex/plans/`
52	- `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-1
53

```
1	# L-008 — Schema migrations are required even on fresh DBs
2	
3	**Class:** Persistence
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (Hermes BUG-002, Critical)
5	**Evidence sources:** Hermes BUG-002 + multiple Hermes/autonomy review issues
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Hermes track widened the `memory_operation_log` table to add `scope`, `workspace_root`, `filename` columns. The change worked on fresh installs because `internal/memory/catalog.go` boots through `storepkg.EnsureSchema`, which created the new shape. But existing databases kept the old five-column table — `EnsureSchema` skipped existing tables — and `agh memory write` failed on upgrade with `no such column: scope`.
10	
11	CodeRabbit flagged it as Critical. The fix: schema migration v6 added through the deterministic migrations runner.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	`EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation has a fundamental gap: it creates tables that don't exist but does not mutate tables that do. Any column/index/constraint addition needs a real migration; a migration is required _even when fresh installs already work_, because upgrade is a first-class scenario in alpha.
16	
17	A second contributor: AGH had two schema paths — the global DB used a real migrations registry, while the catalog DB used `EnsureSchema`. Drift between these paths produced the bug.
18	
19	## Rule
20	
21	> Any change to a SQLite column, index, or constraint MUST add a versioned migration in the migrations registry. `EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation is forbidden for column changes. Test fresh-DB AND reopen-after-restart paths.
22	
23	## Operationalization
24	
25	- **One schema migration primitive shared by all SQLite databases** (`agh.db`, `events.db`, catalog DBs, automation scheduler state, memory operation log).
26	- **Numbered migrations** are recorded in `schema_migrations`. Idempotent. Wrapped in transactions.
27	- **Test the upgrade path:** every schema change must include a `Test*FreshDB` test AND a `Test*ReopenAfterRestart` test.
28	- **`SQLite recovery code paths must rename or remove `-wal`and`-shm`companions, not only the`.db` file.** (Cross-cutting hygiene: see refac-v2 issue 001 Critical WAL recovery bug.)
29	- **Schema-version bump** lives in the migrations registry — do not encode it in code constants.
30	
31	## Allowed exception
32	
33	In greenfield alpha, a hard-cut rename + table rewrite without compat migration is allowed when:
34	
35	1. The change is documented in the techspec's "Delete Targets" section.
36	2. All callers of the old shape are deleted in the same change.
37	3. Per-developer wipe of local SQLite is acceptable cost.
38	
39	## Anti-pattern
40	
41	- `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS new_columns ...` then expecting the table to grow.
42	- Schema-version constants in Go code that don't match the migrations registry.
43	- Tests that only cover fresh-DB.
44	
45	## Source
46	
47	- `.codex/ledger/2026-04-25-MEMORY-hermes-qa-execution.md` (BUG-002)
48	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/reviews-001/issue_020.md` (Critical)
49	- `.compozy/tasks/refac-v2/reviews-001/issue_001.md` (WAL/SHM Critical)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_07.md` (claim/lease schema v7)
51	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L1, `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-2
52	
```

```
1	# L-012 — TechSpec without Go interface signatures triggers heavy review rework
2	
3	**Class:** Spec authoring
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 / 2026-04-26 (autonomy techspec vs. release-adjustments comparison)
5	**Evidence sources:** `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §PRD/TechSpec Quality Patterns
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Two TechSpecs from the same period delivered radically different review trajectories:
10	
11	- **Autonomy techspec** (`.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md`, 706 lines): MVP boundary at top, listed Architectural Boundaries, Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (`ClaimCriteria`, `ClaimedRun`, `TaskClaimer`, `SpawnOpts`, `PermissionNarrower`), data-model fields with rationale, side-table-vs-JSON decisions explicit, lease invariants enumerated as a numbered list. Eighteen tasks executed cleanly with **one** review round.
12	- **Release-adjustments / qa-review** (no `_techspec.md`, just review-only directories): unresolved review queues persisted across multiple PRs. Tasks 07-09 of autonomy that touched contract-laden interfaces had **exactly one** round of fixes because the techspec gave the implementer no contract ambiguity.
13	
14	The differentiator was not length — it was concreteness. Specs that paste signatures, list fields with rationale, and enumerate invariants leave nothing to interpretation. Specs that describe the same mechanics in prose force the implementer to invent shapes that reviewers then reject.
15	
16	## Root cause
17	
18	Prose-only descriptions produce N implementations, where N is the number of agents that read the spec. Reviewers then converge each implementation toward the implicit intent through review rounds — that is the rework. Code blocks (interface signatures, struct fields, SQL DDL, enum values) are unambiguous; reviewers either approve or reject specific tokens, and the spec author resolves the ambiguity once instead of N times.
19	
20	## Rule
21	
22	> A TechSpec is not ready for review until it carries the **six quality markers**:
23	>
24	> 1. MVP boundary statement at top.
25	> 2. Architectural Boundaries section.
26	> 3. Concrete Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (not prose).
27	> 4. Data-model field rationale (purpose + shape per new column / frontmatter field / config key).
28	> 5. Side-table-vs-JSON decision stated for every new domain entity.
29	> 6. Lease / safety invariants as a numbered list.
30	>
31	> Specs without these markers are pre-rejected — they will need multiple review rounds.
32	
33	## Operationalization
34	
35	`cy-spec-peer-review` invokes Opus with a six-marker checklist embedded in the prompt. `cy-spec-preflight` blocks `cy-create-techspec` from completing until the six markers are present.
36	
37	When a spec is missing a marker, fix the spec — do not start tasks against the gap.
38	
39	## Anti-patterns
40	
41	- "The function will accept the relevant config and return the result." (no signature)
42	- "Add a column for ownership tracking." (no rationale, no name, no type)
43	- "Choose the appropriate storage shape." (no decision)
44	- "Ensure the lease is held safely." (no invariants)
45	- "We'll figure out the interface during implementation." (defers the ambiguity)
46	
47	## Source
48	
49	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md` — the cleanest exemplar
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` (lease invariants numbered)
51	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-006.md` (safe spawn caps numbered)
52	- `.compozy/tasks/release-adjustments/` and `.compozy/tasks/qa-review/` — review-only dirs without TechSpecs, persistent unresolved review queues
53	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §"Markers of 'good enough to execute'" and §"Markers of trouble"
54	- `docs/_memory/_synthesis.md` skill candidate S-M9 `agh-techspec-quality-gate`
55	
```

1	# L-014: Runtime Vocabulary Must Match Public Contracts
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture / Product vocabulary
4	
5	## Incident
6	
7	The execution isolation feature was implemented and exposed as "environments" even though the product concept was Sandbox. The mismatch appeared across internal packages, config keys, database columns, API fields, CLI flags, hook topics, extension Host API methods, web navigation, settings UI, generated docs, and task artifacts.
8	
9	The feature was also under-documented: the landing page did not explain Sandbox, runtime docs did not have a dedicated Sandbox section, and the web UI hid the feature under Settings instead of giving it primary navigation.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	The first implementation used an overloaded infrastructure term and let it harden into contracts before product vocabulary was settled. Once code, docs, generated references, and QA artifacts diverged, the feature became difficult to discover and easy to misrepresent.
14	
15	## Fix / Rule
16	
17	Public runtime concepts need one canonical noun before they reach contracts. If the noun changes during greenfield alpha, perform one hard cut across every public and internal surface in the same change:
18	
19	- runtime packages, storage columns, config keys, generated contracts, CLI flags, hook names, Host API methods, logs, and tests
20	- web routes, navigation, page copy, mocks, route tests, and generated client types
21	- landing pages, docs navigation, runtime guides, generated CLI docs, and examples
22	- `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md`, `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts, glossary, and lessons
23	
24	Do not keep aliases, redirects, dual fields, or fallback parsing for the obsolete product noun. Generic operating-system terminology remains valid when it describes process environment variables or host context.
25	
26	## Evidence
27	
28	- Accepted implementation plan: `.codex/plans/2026-04-28-sandbox-hard-cut.md`
29	- Runtime package hard cut: `internal/sandbox/`
30	- Public API/schema source: `internal/api/spec/spec.go`
31	- Web IA hard cut: `web/src/routes/_app/sandbox.tsx` and `web/src/components/app-sidebar.tsx`
32	- Dedicated docs: `packages/site/content/runtime/core/sandbox/index.mdx`
33	- Landing page surface: `packages/site/components/landing/sandbox-section.tsx`
34

1	# L-003 — `task_runs` is the single durable work queue
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture / Autonomy
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy techspec, ADR-003)
5	**Evidence sources:** 3 ADRs forbid duplication
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Designing the autonomy MVP, several alternatives proposed a parallel scheduler-owned queue ("scheduler claims, then asks task service to assign"). All were rejected.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	Two durable queues for the same work creates two sources of truth for ownership state. Any divergence (lease vs. claim, sweep vs. heartbeat, recovery vs. boot) becomes a race. The existing `task_runs` table already carries `status`, `attempt`, `idempotency_key`, `origin_kind/ref` — adding state via columns and side tables is strictly cheaper than adding a parallel table.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> `task_runs` is the single durable work queue. Do not introduce a parallel queue or actor table. Add new ownership/state via columns + side tables on `task_runs`.
18	
19	## Lease invariants (operationalization)
20	
21	1. Exactly one active claim token per non-terminal run.
22	2. Heartbeat/complete/fail/release compare run owner + claim token.
23	3. Stale/late after recovery fails explicitly (no silent reassignment).
24	4. Sweep + heartbeat serialize via SQLite tx (`BEGIN IMMEDIATE`).
25	5. Boot recovery runs BEFORE the scheduler accepts wake/claim traffic.
26	6. Lease extension is bounded by config.
27	7. One active lease per session in MVP.
28	
29	## Side-table strategy
30	
31	- Capability matching = exact-match rows in `task_run_required_capabilities` and `task_run_preferred_capabilities`.
32	- Coordination channels: `coordination_channel_id` column on `task_runs`.
33	- Permission narrowing data: side table indexed by `task_run_id`.
34	- **Never** stuff dynamic ownership/match state into a JSON metadata blob.
35	
36	## Source
37	
38	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` — Extend Task Runs for Atomic Claim/Lease
39	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
40	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-010.md` — Manual Operator Control First-Class
41	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` (lesson 1, with 3 ADRs cited)
42	
```

1	# L-007 — E2E harness regressions follow runtime contract changes
2	
3	**Class:** Testing
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_18 QA)
5	**Evidence sources:** task_18 BUG-001/002/003 + global_runs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The autonomy MVP `make verify` passed. Real-scenario QA via `real-scenario-qa` then surfaced three Playwright/E2E regressions:
10	
11	- **BUG-001** — workspace onboarding race in the web E2E `TC-AUTO-015` test; absent shared workspace-onboarding wait helper.
12	- **BUG-002** — `acpmock` exact-match canonicalization for situation-augmented prompts. Task 04 added a situation-context augmenter that changed the rendered prompt; the deterministic ACP mock fixture matcher still expected the pre-Task-04 shape.
13	- **BUG-003** — Tasks browser E2E asserting an empty Agents-panel state; manual-first publish actually rendered an active run.
14	
15	All three were rooted in tests written against an _older_ runtime contract. None was a production bug.
16	
17	## Root cause
18	
19	When a runtime contract changes — a new prompt augmenter, a different fixture canonicalization, a new manual-first UI state — the deterministic test infrastructure (acpmock fixtures, Playwright selectors, browser fixtures) embeds the _old_ contract. `make verify` passes because tests still hit their old expectations. Real-scenario QA exposes the drift.
20	
21	## Rule
22	
23	> When a runtime contract changes (prompt augmenter, situation context, fixture format, manual-first UI state), the E2E mock and matchers ship in the same PR. Do not let the test infrastructure encode a stale contract.
24	
25	## Operationalization
26	
27	- For ACP fixture work: replace fragile string-matching with structured prompt metadata. acpmock uses typed metadata, not rendered prompt substrings.
28	- For Playwright E2E: add shared wait helpers (`web/e2e/fixtures/selectors.ts`) for workspace onboarding, session creation, manual-first publish. New runtime states require helper updates in the same PR.
29	- Real-scenario QA is the canonical regression net. `make verify` is necessary but not sufficient.
30	- E2E regressions surfaced in the QA pass are NOT production bugs unless they reveal divergent runtime behavior. Fix the test infrastructure, not the runtime.
31	
32	## Anti-pattern
33	
34	- Adding a `time.Sleep(2 * time.Second)` to "stabilize" a flaky Playwright spec.
35	- Loosening an acpmock matcher to substring instead of metadata.
36	- Skipping E2E in the QA pass because "the unit tests cover it."
37	
38	## Source
39	
40	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_18.md`
41	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/verification-report.md`
42	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/issues/BUG-001.md`, `BUG-002.md`, `BUG-003.md`
43	- `.codex/plans/2026-04-17-e2e-confidence-hardening.md` — root-cause plan for the structured-metadata switch
44	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` (task_18 findings), `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` (autonomy QA section)
45
